serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Shui Jing Bao is one to two pieces, each weighing approximately 40–50 g, as commonly served at dim sum or hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Shui Jing Bao — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Shui Jing Bao (steamed dumpling with turnip and carrot filling), CR ID F14080030. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Shui Jing Bao. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: steamed wheat-starch dim sum dumplings (har gow / crystal skin dumplings) with a translucent wheat starch wrapper and vegetable filling. Wheat starch-based steamed dumplings have a GI in the range of 60–70 per University of Sydney GI Database analogues for wheat-starch dim sum items. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (45 g × 22 g/100 g = 9.9 g) / 100 = 6.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 409 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Shui Jing Bao to one piece per sitting and avoid sodium-rich dipping sauces such as soy sauce, as the combined sodium load can meaningfully elevate blood pressure in sodium-sensitive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 22 g/100 g with moderate GI (~65) from wheat starch wrapper; partially offset by 2.5 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Shui Jing Bao in controlled portions of one to two pieces, pairing with non-starchy vegetables and avoiding sugary dipping sauces to moderate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 409 mg/100 g is elevated; fat content 4.8 g/100 g is moderate; saturated fat breakdown not available from HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should restrict portion size to one piece and request reduced-salt preparation, as the sodium content at 409 mg/100 g contributes meaningfully to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg recommended by cardiovascular guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shui Jing Bao (水晶包, literally 'crystal bun') is a traditional Singaporean Chinese dim sum item consisting of a translucent, semi-transparent steamed wrapper made from wheat starch encasing a savoury filling of shredded turnip (radish) and carrot, seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. The wrapper becomes glossy and slightly chewy when steamed, giving the dumpling its characteristic crystal-like appearance. The filling is mildly sweet from the carrot and turnip, with a tender, slightly crunchy texture. Shui Jing Bao is commonly served at dim sum restaurants, hawker centres, and breakfast stalls across Singapore and is associated with Cantonese dim sum culture. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 141 kcal, 2.4 g protein, 4.8 g fat, 22 g carbohydrate, 2.5 g dietary fibre, and 409 mg sodium. The moderate carbohydrate content and medium glycemic index (~65) make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content at 409 mg/100 g is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The vegetable filling contributes dietary fibre and micronutrients including calcium (29 mg/100 g) and iron (0.8 mg/100 g). The dish is suitable for vegetarians and is halal-friendly when prepared without lard.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Shui Jing Bao?
Is Shui Jing Bao suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F14080030) at confidence 0.95. The food identity is exact. GI is estimated from analogous wheat-starch dim sum items via University of Sydney GI Database analogues, which reduces GI confidence to medium; however, overall record confidence remains high given the exact HPB nutrient match.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: the HPB Foodo database entry for Shui Jing Bao (F14080030) does not report detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, or moisture data. These fields are set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene rules. The input record's health_suitability section referenced 'pork filling' and 'arachidonic acid', which is inconsistent with the food description ('steamed dumpling with turnip and carrot filling'). The description and dietary_tags ('vegetarian', 'plant_based') confirm this is a vegetable-filled dumpling with no pork. The rheumatoid_arthritis and diabetes health notes referencing pork have been corrected accordingly. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-starch steamed dim sum dumplings from University of Sydney GI Database analogues; no direct GI measurement for Shui Jing Bao was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 45 g serving size: GL = 65 × (45 × 0.22) / 100 = 6.4.
